title: Quantum Advantage in Multi Agent Reinforcement Learning
abstract: We present an empirical evaluation of quantum entanglement in agent coordination within quantum multi agent reinforcement learning (QMARL). While QMARL has attracted growing interest recently, most prior work evaluates quantum policies without provable baselines, making it impossible to rigorously distinguish quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidence. We address this directly by evaluating a decentralized QMARL framework with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors with shared entangled states. In the CHSH game, which has a mathematically proven classical performance ceiling of 0.75 win rate, we show that entangled QMARL agents approach the Tsirelson limit of 0.854, providing clear evidence of their quantum advantage. We show that unentangled quantum circuits match the classical baseline, confirming that entanglement and not the quantum circuit itself is the active coordination mechanism. We also explore the effect of specific entanglement structures, as some Bell states enable coordination gains while others actively harm performance. On cooperative navigation (CoopNav), QMARL without entanglement achieves $\sim2\times$ improvement in success rate over classical MAA2C ($\sim$0.85 versus $\sim$0.40), with the hybrid configuration, quantum actor paired with a classical centralised critic, outperforming both fully classical and fully quantum solutions. We present our experimental analysis and discuss future work.
summary: This paper explores the use of quantum entanglement to enable coordination between independent agents in reinforcement learning. It proves that entanglement allows agents to break classical performance bounds in specific games, while also identifying that quantum circuit expressiveness can drive advantages in other navigation tasks.
main_problem: Investigating whether quantum entanglement can serve as a coordination mechanism in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) and distinguishing genuine quantum advantage from mere increases in model capacity.
main_result: The study demonstrates that entangled agents can exceed classical performance limits in the CHSH game by approaching the Tsirelson limit, while in navigation tasks, the advantage stems from VQC expressiveness and hybrid architectures rather than entanglement.
method: The authors use a Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) framework, comparing classical MAA2C, unentangled QMARL, and entangled QMARL using Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) across various benchmarks.
model_or_system: The model utilizes Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) as policy networks for agents, employing Bell and GHZ states for entanglement, and is tested in the CHSH game, CoinGame, and Cooperative Navigation (CoopNav) environments.
key_observables: Win rates in CHSH, expected total discounted reward, success rate, collision count, and episode length.
important_parameters: Tsirelson limit (0.854), classical CHSH ceiling (0.75), qubit count (n_q), circuit depth (d), and entropy regularization coefficient (beta).
main_assumptions: Agents are cooperative and operate under strictly decentralized execution without runtime communication; simulations are conducted in a noiseless statevector environment.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the QMARL CTDE framework; Figure 3 compares performance in CoinGame; Figure 4 shows CoopNav success rates and collisions; Figure 7 illustrates win rates approaching the Tsirelson bound; Figure 8 shows qubit/depth ablation studies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QMARL framework, defines the quantum and classical architectures, details the experimental environments (CHSH, CoinGame, CoopNav), presents results for entanglement-based coordination and VQC expressiveness, performs ablation studies on circuit parameters, and concludes with a parameter-parity analysis.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: QMARL under CTDE: Agents execute decentralised quantum policies independently; a centralised critic trains all agents using global state and joint actions.

Figure 2: CHSH win rate versus training steps with std dev bands. (a) Effect of each of the four Bell states (|Φ−⟩, |Φ+⟩, |Ψ−⟩, |Ψ+⟩). (b) Win rate by input pair for all five QMARL variants. The horizontal lines mark the proven classical bound (0.75) and the Tsirelson bound (0.854).

Figure 2 shows win rate versus training steps with standard deviation bands for classical MARL, QMARL without entanglement and QMARL with entanglement variants. Classical MARL converges at and below the classical bound of 0.75. QMARL without entanglement tracks the classical baseline throughout, confirming that the quantum circuit alone provides no coordination advantage. Only entangled QMARL variants consistently exceeds 0.75 and approaches the Tsirelson bound of 0.854, establishing that entanglement is the active mechanism driving quantum advantage.
Figure 3: CoinGame: Classical MARL versus QMARL without entanglement (top) and across Bell states (bottom).

Figure 4 compares Classical MAA2C with unentangled QMARL and entangled QMARL variants on CoopNav. The unentangled QMARL variant achieves the strongest performance across the main task metrics, whereas Classical MAA2C improves more slowly and but plateaus at a lower final success
